title: Standard FFN
description: The default dense transformer MLP that expands, activates, and projects each token state after attention.
openingSummary: A standard FFN is the default dense MLP after attention in a transformer block, using an expand-activate-project path so each token gets the same per-position transformation.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A standard FFN is the default dense feed-forward block used in most transformer layers. After attention updates a token's hidden state, the standard FFN sends that vector through one wide linear layer, applies a pointwise activation, and projects it back to the model width.

whatItOptimizes.title: What It Optimizes
whatItOptimizes.body: The standard FFN optimizes for a simple, uniform dense path after attention. Every token uses the same parameters and the same full hidden width, which keeps the block easy to reason about and easy to compare against later variants.

practicalBenefit.title: Practical Benefit
practicalBenefit.body: This is the baseline most transformer readers see in diagrams, code, and papers. Once you know the dense baseline, it becomes much easier to spot what later FFN variants are actually changing.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: The token state enters one shared dense block, expands into a wider hidden width, passes through an activation, and then projects back to the model width. No router chooses between experts, and no separate gate branch changes which hidden features stay active.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: The dense FFN formula below is the baseline for the whole family. It shows the single shared expand, activate, and project path that many later variants start from.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: Compared with other FFN-family modules, the standard FFN keeps the most direct dense path. The main tradeoff is that every token activates the full hidden block every time.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: Many early and still-common transformer stacks use the standard dense FFN as the default post-attention module.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: The standard FFN activates the whole hidden path for every token, so it cannot add expert capacity sparsely the way MoE does. It also lacks the extra gating flexibility that later dense variants such as SwiGLU introduce.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: Even when a paper introduces a new FFN variant, the standard FFN remains the reference point that explains what changed. It is the baseline shape many readers still need first.

computeFlow.alt: Standard FFN graph showing one dense expand, activate, and project path for each token
computeFlow.caption: A standard FFN keeps one dense path for every token: widen the hidden state, apply an activation, then project back to model width

comparisonTable.caption: How the standard FFN compares with nearby FFN variants

standard.pathShape: One dense expand -> activate -> project path
standard.activeCompute: All hidden units in one shared MLP
standard.mainTradeoff: Simple and predictable, but every token pays for the full block

swiglu.pathShape: Value branch multiplied by a SiLU gate branch
swiglu.activeCompute: All hidden units plus a learned gate
swiglu.mainTradeoff: More expressive, but the block is less minimal than the dense baseline

moe.pathShape: Router chooses a few expert MLPs, then merges them
moe.activeCompute: Only top-k experts activate for each token
moe.mainTradeoff: Much more capacity, but routing and load balancing add complexity

ht.definition: Hidden state for token position t.

w1.definition: Projection into the wide hidden layer.

sigma.definition: Pointwise activation inside the hidden layer.

ht.definition: Hidden state for token position t.

wv.definition: Projection that builds the value branch.

wg.definition: Projection that builds the gate branch.

wo.definition: Projection back to model width after gating.

odot.definition: Elementwise multiplication between the value and gate branches.

allUnitsActive.label: All wide hidden units activate for every token

noRouterNote.label: No router and no expert choice: every token uses the same dense block

denseNote.label: Every token uses the same dense expand -> activate -> project path
